title: R Foundations for Economics
description: Essential R code snippets for basic operations, vectors, functions, and simple economic models

snippets.title: Arithmetic Operations
snippets.code:
# Basic arithmetic
2 + 3          # Addition: 5
10 - 4         # Subtraction: 6
5 * 6          # Multiplication: 30
20 / 4         # Division: 5
2^5            # Exponentiation: 32
17 %% 5        # Modulo (remainder): 2
17 %/% 5       # Integer division: 3
snippets.notes: R follows standard order of operations (PEMDAS). Use parentheses to override.

snippets.title: Mathematical Functions
snippets.code:
# Square root and absolute value
sqrt(16)       # 4
abs(-5)        # 5

# Exponential and logarithms
exp(1)         # e = 2.718282
log(10)        # Natural log: 2.302585
log10(100)     # Log base 10: 2
log(8, base=2) # Log base 2: 3

# Constants
pi             # 3.141593
snippets.notes: log() is natural logarithm by default. Use log10() for base-10 or specify base=
common_errors: Assuming log() is base-10 (it's natural log)

snippets.title: Variable Assignment
snippets.code:
# Assignment (use <- or =)
price <- 25
quantity <- 100
revenue <- price * quantity
revenue        # 2500

# View all variables
ls()

# Remove variables
rm(price)              # Remove one
rm(list = ls())        # Remove ALL (careful!)
snippets.notes: Convention: use <- for assignment. Variable names are case-sensitive.

snippets.title: Creating Vectors
snippets.code:
# Combine elements with c()
prices <- c(250, 275, 300, 280, 290)
crops <- c("wheat", "corn", "soybean")

# Sequences
years <- 2020:2025                      # Integer sequence
by_two <- seq(0, 10, by = 2)            # 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10
five_points <- seq(0, 100, length.out = 5)  # 0, 25, 50, 75, 100

# Repetition
zeros <- rep(0, 5)                      # 0, 0, 0, 0, 0
pattern <- rep(c(1, 2), times = 3)      # 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2
snippets.notes: c() combines elements. seq() creates sequences. rep() creates repetitions.
common_errors: Confusing times= and each= in rep()

snippets.title: Vector Operations
snippets.code:
x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
y <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)

# Element-wise operations
x + y          # 11, 22, 33, 44, 55
x * y          # 10, 40, 90, 160, 250
x * 2          # 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 (scalar recycling)

# Summary functions
length(x)      # 5
sum(x)         # 15
mean(x)        # 3
sd(x)          # 1.581
min(x)         # 1
max(x)         # 5
snippets.notes: Operations are element-wise. Scalars are recycled to match vector length.
common_errors: Expecting matrix multiplication with * (use %*% for that)

snippets.title: Indexing and Subsetting
snippets.code:
prices <- c(250, 275, 300, 280, 290)

# Positive indexing (select)
prices[1]           # First element: 250 (R is 1-indexed!)
prices[c(1, 3, 5)]  # Elements 1, 3, 5
prices[2:4]         # Elements 2 through 4

# Negative indexing (exclude)
prices[-1]          # All except first

# Logical indexing
prices[prices > 280]  # Elements > 280: 300, 290

# Which indices?
which(prices > 280)   # Returns: 3, 5
which.max(prices)     # Index of max: 3
snippets.notes: R uses 1-based indexing (first element is [1], not [0]). Negative indices exclude.

snippets.title: Creating Functions
snippets.notes: Functions use function() keyword. Default arguments go after required ones.
common_errors: Forgetting return() (last expression returned by default)

snippets.title: Demand and Supply Functions
snippets.description: Define linear demand and supply curves
snippets.notes: pmax() ensures non-negative quantities (parallel maximum with 0).
snippets.economic_interpretation: Demand slopes down (b > 0 gives negative slope). Supply slopes up (d > 0).

snippets.title: Finding Equilibrium
snippets.code:
# Excess demand function
excess_demand <- function(P) {
  demand(P) - supply(P)
}

# Find P where excess demand = 0
equilibrium <- uniroot(excess_demand, interval = c(0, 100))
P_star <- equilibrium$root
Q_star <- demand(P_star)

cat("Equilibrium: P* =", round(P_star, 2), 
    ", Q* =", round(Q_star, 2))
snippets.notes: uniroot() finds where function equals zero. interval= specifies search range.
snippets.economic_interpretation: Equilibrium where Qd = Qs. Excess demand = 0 means market clears.

snippets.title: Market Visualization
snippets.code:
# Generate data
P_range <- seq(0, 50, by = 0.5)
Qd <- demand(P_range)
Qs <- supply(P_range)

# Plot
plot(Qd, P_range, type = "l", col = "blue", lwd = 2,
     xlim = c(0, 250), ylim = c(0, 60),
     xlab = "Quantity", ylab = "Price",
     main = "Supply and Demand")
lines(Qs, P_range, col = "red", lwd = 2)

# Mark equilibrium
points(Q_star, P_star, pch = 19, cex = 1.5)

# Add reference lines
abline(h = P_star, lty = 2, col = "gray")
abline(v = Q_star, lty = 2, col = "gray")

# Legend and grid
legend("topright", c("Demand", "Supply"), 
       col = c("blue", "red"), lwd = 2)
grid()
snippets.notes: type='l' for line. lwd controls line width. pch controls point shape.

snippets.title: Elasticity Calculation
snippets.notes: Elasticity is negative for demand. |ε| > 1 is elastic, |ε| < 1 is inelastic.
snippets.economic_interpretation: Inelastic demand: price increases raise total revenue. Elastic demand: price increases reduce revenue.

snippets.title: Tax Analysis
snippets.description: Analyze effect of per-unit tax on suppliers
snippets.code:
tax <- 5

# Supply with tax: suppliers receive (P - tax)
supply_tax <- function(P, c = -20, d = 6, t = tax) {
  Qs <- c + d * (P - t)
  return(pmax(Qs, 0))
}

# New equilibrium
excess_tax <- function(P) demand(P) - supply_tax(P)
eq_tax <- uniroot(excess_tax, c(0, 100))
P_consumer <- eq_tax$root
Q_new <- demand(P_consumer)
P_producer <- P_consumer - tax

# Tax burden
consumer_burden <- P_consumer - P_star
producer_burden <- P_star - P_producer

cat("Consumer pays: $", round(consumer_burden, 2), 
    " (", round(100*consumer_burden/tax), "%)\n")
cat("Producer pays: $", round(producer_burden, 2),
    " (", round(100*producer_burden/tax), "%)\n")

# Deadweight loss
DWL <- 0.5 * tax * (Q_star - Q_new)
cat("DWL: $", round(DWL, 2))
snippets.notes: Tax creates wedge between consumer price and producer price.
snippets.economic_interpretation: Tax burden depends on relative elasticities. More inelastic side bears more burden.

snippets.title: Data Frames Preview
snippets.description: Create and manipulate basic data frames
snippets.code:
# Create data frame
farm_data <- data.frame(
  crop = c("wheat", "corn", "soybean"),
  yield = c(45, 180, 50),
  price = c(7.50, 4.50, 13.00)
)

# Access columns
farm_data$yield        # By name
farm_data[, "yield"]   # Same
farm_data[, 2]         # By position

# Add column
farm_data$revenue <- farm_data$yield * farm_data$price

# Filter rows
farm_data[farm_data$yield > 50, ]
snippets.notes: Data frames are tables. Access columns with $ or [,]. Filter rows with logical conditions.

common_patterns.template:
excess <- function(P) demand(P) - supply(P)
eq <- uniroot(excess, c(lower, upper))
P_star <- eq$root

plot(x1, y1, type='l', col='blue')
lines(x2, y2, col='red')
legend('topright', c('Curve1', 'Curve2'), col=c('blue','red'), lty=1)

debugging_tips.cause: Variable not defined or typo in name
debugging_tips.fix: Check spelling and that variable was created

debugging_tips.cause: Function name misspelled or package not loaded
debugging_tips.fix: Check spelling; load package with library()

debugging_tips.cause: Trying to do math on non-numeric data
debugging_tips.fix: Check data types with class(); convert if needed
Check that your calculation produces output
